[406]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 家人匆遽解维 - Describes the hurried action of untying the mooring ropes. '匆遽' means 'hurriedly,' and '解维' means 'to untie the ropes.'
  • 传语止王先生 - '传语' means 'to convey a message,' and '止' means 'to stop' or 'to detain.'
  • 老父得先生刀圭，乃者熟寝，病若脱矣 - '刀圭' is a unit of medicine, and '乃者' means 'then' or 'at that time.' '熟寝' means 'deep sleep,' and '病若脱矣' means 'the illness has vanished.'
  • 父存；先生去，父且大去 - '父存' means 'the father is alive,' and '大去' means 'to die.'
  • 惟先生终哀怜之 - '惟' means 'only' or 'just,' and '哀怜' means 'to pity' or 'to show compassion.'
  • 自疑曰 - '自疑' means 'to doubt oneself.'
  • 必绐我 - '必' means 'certainly,' and '绐' means 'to deceive.'
  • 强随之登堂 - '强随' means 'to follow reluctantly,' and '登堂' means 'to enter the hall.'
  • 心犹怦怦然 - '怦怦然' describes a rapid heartbeat, often associated with anxiety or excitement.
  • 申谢再三 - '申谢' means 'to express gratitude repeatedly,' and '再三' means 'again and again.'
  • 先生用药何神验乃尔 - '何神验' means 'how miraculous,' and '乃尔' means 'like this.'
  • 昨已得其概 - '得其概' means 'to grasp the general idea.'
  • 索药渣观之 - '索' means 'to ask for,' and '药渣' refers to the residue of medicine.
  • 幸不误 - '幸' means 'fortunately,' and '不误' means 'not mistaken.'
  • 更与数剂 - '更与' means 'to give more,' and '数剂' means 'several doses.'
  • 厚获而归 - '厚获' means 'to gain greatly,' and '而归' means 'and return.'
  • 人呼为醉先生云 - '呼为' means 'to call,' and '醉先生' is a nickname, possibly referring to someone who is drunk or foolish.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 秋泉 - The name of the protagonist, likely a doctor or healer.
  • 主人 - The host or patient's family, who is ill.
  • 老父 - The patient, referred to as 'the old father.'
  • 刀圭 - A unit of medicine, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
  • 熟寝 - Deep sleep, often associated with recovery or healing.
  • 大去 - A euphemism for death.
  • 申谢 - Expressing gratitude, a common practice in traditional Chinese culture.
  • 厚获 - Gaining greatly, often in terms of wealth or recognition.

Proper Nouns

  • 秋泉 - The name of the doctor or healer.
  • 主人 - The host or patient's family.
  • 老父 - The patient, referred to as 'the old father.'
  • 王先生 - A title or name, likely referring to the doctor or a respected individual.

[407]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - A conventional phrase used to introduce a commentary or reflection, often by a historian or scholar.
  • 此事見《烏青志》 - A formal statement indicating that the event is recorded in the *Wuqing Zhì* (Records of Wuqing), a historical or local chronicle.
  • 醉夢之中 - Literally 'in a drunken dream,' used to describe a state of altered consciousness or a surreal experience.
  • 藥之神效 - Refers to the miraculous efficacy of medicine.
  • 豈其中有鬼神耶 - A rhetorical question expressing doubt or skepticism about the presence of supernatural forces.
  • 生死之自有命 - A philosophical statement suggesting that life and death are determined by fate or destiny.
  • 一笑 - A literary device often used to indicate a wry or ironic tone, implying that the matter is trivial or absurd.
  • 《水經注》 - A famous geographical and historical work by Li Daoyuan, which provides detailed descriptions of rivers and related legends.
  • 交趾金溪究山 - Refers to a specific location in ancient China, likely in the region of Jiaozhi (modern-day northern Vietnam).
  • 大蛇，名曰蚺蛇 - Describes a large snake, specifically the *rán shé* (蚺蛇), a type of python.
  • 長十丈，圍七八尺 - Provides a vivid description of the snake's size, emphasizing its enormity.
  • 常在樹上伺鹿獸 - Describes the snake's behavior of waiting in trees to ambush deer or other animals.
  • 鹿獸過，輒低頭繞之 - Describes the snake's method of attacking prey by lowering its head and coiling around the animal.
  • 有頃，濡濕訖 - Indicates a short period of time during which the snake moistens its prey.
  • 便食頭角 - Describes the snake's consumption of the prey's head and horns.
  • 三月骨皆鑽皮出 - Suggests that after three months, the bones of the prey emerge through the snake's skin.
  • 山夷始見蛇不動時 - Refers to the local people (山夷) seeing the snake when it is motionless.
  • 以竹簽簽蛇頭至尾 - Describes the method of killing the snake by inserting a bamboo stake from head to tail.
  • 殺而食之 - Indicates the act of killing and eating the snake.
  • 以為珍異 - Suggests that the snake is considered a delicacy or something rare and valuable.
  • 一說以婦人衣投之 - Refers to an alternative method of capturing the snake by throwing a woman's garment at it.
  • 則蟠而不起走 - Describes the snake's reaction of coiling up and remaining still instead of fleeing.
  • 便可得也 - Indicates that the snake can then be easily captured.
  • 楊氏南裔《異物志》 - Refers to a work titled *Yì Wù Zhì* (Record of Strange Things) by Yang, likely a southern scholar.
  • 蚺惟大蛇，既宏且長 - Describes the *rán shé* as a large and long snake.
  • 采色驳荦 - Refers to the snake's varied and colorful appearance.
  • 其文錦章 - Describes the snake's skin as having a pattern like brocade.
  • 食猪吞鹿 - Indicates that the snake eats pigs and swallows deer whole.
  • 腴成養創 - Suggests that the snake's fat is used to heal wounds.
  • 賓享嘉宴，是豆是觞 - Refers to the snake's fat being served at banquets as a delicacy.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 外史氏 - A title often used by historians or scholars to introduce their commentary, similar to 'the historian says.'
  • 《烏青志》 - A historical or local chronicle, possibly a regional record of events or legends.
  • 《水經注》 - A classic geographical and historical work by Li Daoyuan, which provides detailed descriptions of rivers and related legends.
  • 交趾金溪究山 - A specific location in ancient China, likely in the region of Jiaozhi (modern-day northern Vietnam).
  • 山夷 - Refers to the local people or indigenous inhabitants of the area, often used to describe non-Han ethnic groups.
  • 《異物志》 - A work that records strange or unusual creatures, often found in ancient texts.
  • 楊氏 - Likely refers to a southern scholar or author, possibly from the southern regions of China.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This phrase introduces the source of the story but does not specify the exact nature of the event.
  • This sentence combines a surreal experience with a description of the miraculous effects of medicine, creating a contrast between the real and the fantastical.
  • This rhetorical question introduces a skeptical tone, questioning the role of supernatural forces.
  • This clause contrasts the limitations of medical knowledge with the inevitability of fate.
  • This final phrase adds a layer of irony or humor, suggesting that the entire matter is trivial or absurd.
  • This sentence introduces a detailed description of a legendary snake, combining factual information with mythological elements.
  • This clause describes the snake's hunting behavior in a vivid and dramatic way.
  • This sequence of actions describes the snake's method of consuming its prey in a step-by-step manner.
  • This phrase suggests a supernatural or exaggerated element, indicating that the snake's prey remains inside for an extended period.
  • This sentence describes a method of killing the snake, emphasizing the local people's knowledge and practices.
  • This phrase suggests that the snake is highly valued, possibly due to its rarity or the cultural significance of its consumption.
  • This alternative method of capturing the snake introduces a cultural belief or superstition.
  • This phrase introduces a different source, providing additional context and information about the snake.
  • This description emphasizes the snake's size and grandeur.
  • This clause describes the snake's appearance in a poetic and vivid way.
  • This phrase highlights the snake's diet and the practical use of its fat.
  • This final clause describes the cultural significance of the snake's fat in banquets and feasts.

Proper Nouns

  • 《烏青志》 - A historical or local chronicle.
  • 《水經注》 - A classic geographical and historical work by Li Daoyuan.
  • 交趾金溪究山 - A specific location in ancient China.
  • 《異物志》 - A work that records strange or unusual creatures.
  • 楊氏 - A southern scholar or author.

[408]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 蚺蛇大者，能吞鹿食人 - Describes the large python's ability to swallow deer and humans, emphasizing its size and ferocity.
  • 性极淫 - Refers to the snake's extreme sexual nature, which is a metaphorical way of describing its behavior.
  • 取妇人敝袴掷地，以首戴之，俯仰顿撼甚乐 - Describes the snake's behavior of picking up a woman's discarded trousers and wearing them, suggesting a form of play or pleasure.
  • 度其出入之地 - Means to estimate the areas where the snake moves in and out.
  • 先钉罗桩数行 - Refers to setting up rows of stakes or poles to trap the snake.
  • 狭仅容其身 - Means the space is narrow enough to accommodate only the snake's body.
  • 蛇身既巨，到狭处曲折转身不便 - Explains that the snake's large size makes it difficult to turn in narrow spaces.
  • 皮肉皆缩 - Means the snake's skin and flesh shrink or contract with each strike.
  • 有一泡，死而血凝 - Refers to a bubble or sac that, when burst, causes the snake to die due to blood coagulation.
  • 此《岭南杂记》所言，与《水经注》合 - Indicates that this account is consistent with descriptions in *Lingnan Zaji* and *Shui Jing Zhu*.
  • 惟《桂海虞衡志》言 - Introduces a different account from *Gui Hai Yu Heng Zhi*.
  • 常出逐鹿食之 - Describes the snake's habit of chasing and eating deer.
  • 寨兵善捕之 - Refers to the skill of the village guards in capturing the snake.
  • 数辈满头插花 - Describes the guards wearing flowers in their hair.
  • 蛇喜花，必注视 - Indicates that the snake is attracted to the flowers and will look at them.
  • 大呼“红娘子” - Refers to the guards shouting 'Red Nanny,' a term that may have cultural significance.
  • 蛇益俯其首不动 - Means the snake lowers its head even more and remains still.
  • 壮士大刀断其首 - Describes a brave man cutting off the snake's head with a large knife.
  • 众悉奔散，远伺之 - Means the guards scatter and observe from a distance.
  • 有顷，蛇奋迅腾掷 - Describes the snake's sudden and vigorous movements.
  • 道旁小木尽拔 - Means the small trees along the path are all uprooted.
  • 力竭乃毙 - Indicates that the snake dies when it exhausts its strength.
  • 一村饱其肉 - Means the entire village feasts on the snake's meat.
  • 然石湖所志，率经亲历，必非无据 - Concludes that the accounts from Shi Hu are based on personal experience and are therefore reliable.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 蚺蛇 - Refers to a large python, often associated with dangerous and mythical creatures in Chinese folklore.
  • 《岭南杂记》 - *Lingnan Zaji* is a collection of notes and observations about the Lingnan region, known for its rich biodiversity and exotic animals.
  • 《水经注》 - *Shui Jing Zhu* is a famous geographical and historical text that describes rivers and their surroundings, including various animals and plants.
  • 《桂海虞衡志》 - *Gui Hai Yu Heng Zhi* is another historical text that focuses on the natural and cultural aspects of the Guihai region.
  • 红娘子 - 'Red Nanny' is a term that may have cultural significance, possibly referring to a specific figure or symbol in the region.

Proper Nouns

  • 《岭南杂记》 - *Lingnan Zaji* (Notes on Lingnan)
  • 《水经注》 - *Shui Jing Zhu* (Commentary on the Waterways Classic)
  • 《桂海虞衡志》 - *Gui Hai Yu Heng Zhi* (Records of Guihai's Natural and Cultural Aspects)
  • 红娘子 - 'Red Nanny' (a term with cultural significance)

[409]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 又按 - Transitional phrase meaning 'furthermore' or 'additionally,' used to introduce further evidence or commentary.
  • 其信然耶 - Raises a question about the truth of the matter, indicating doubt or uncertainty.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 普安州 - A specific region, likely in the context of the text, possibly in southern China.
  • 五月五日 - The fifth day of the fifth lunar month, significant in Chinese culture, often associated with the Dragon Boat Festival.
  • 蚺蛇 - Refers to a specific type of snake, likely the king cobra or another large snake species, often associated with venom and danger.
  • 养蛇户 - Refers to households or families that raise snakes, a practice that may have been common in certain regions for medicinal or other purposes.
  • 上贡 - Refers to the practice of offering tribute to a higher authority, often the emperor or a ruling dynasty.
  • 曝干 - Refers to the traditional method of drying medicinal substances in the sun, a common practice in traditional Chinese medicine.

Proper Nouns

  • 《岭表录异》 - A specific text or record, likely a historical or geographical work.
  • 普安州 - A specific region, likely in the context of the text.
  • 蚺蛇 - A specific type of snake, likely the king cobra or another large snake species.

[410]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 南裔《异物志》 - A reference to a classical Chinese text, *The Records of Strange Things from the Southern Regions*, which documents unusual people, animals, and customs from southern China.
  • 蚺蛇牙长六七寸 - The fang of the python is six to seven inches long.
  • 土人尤重之 - The local people value it especially.
  • 云辟不祥 - They say it wards off misfortune.
  • 利远行 - It is beneficial for long journeys.
  • 卖一枚，值牛数头 - Selling one [fang] is worth several oxen.
  • 采龙眼 - Picking lychees.
  • 龙眼枝甚柔脆 - The lychee branches are very soft and brittle.
  • 熟时赁惯手登采 - When ripe, they hire skilled climbers to pick them.
  • 恐其恣啖 - To prevent them from eating freely.
  • 树叶扶疏，人坐绿阴中 - The leaves are sparse and spread out; people sit in the green shade.
  • 高低断续，喁喁不已 - High and low, broken and continuous, the singing never ceases.
  • 偶听颇足娱耳 - Occasionally listening is quite pleasant to the ear.
  • 细思之，令人欲笑 - Upon careful thought, it makes one want to laugh.
  • 少读《王莽传》 - When I was young, I read the biography of Wang Mang.
  • 剽摹古籍 - Plagiarized or imitated ancient texts.
  • 以恣粉饰 - To indulge in embellishment.
  • 不独仿《大诰》等著作也 - Not only did he imitate works like the *Da Gao*.
  • 想见当时居之不疑，如醉如痴之状 - One can imagine that at the time, they lived in this without doubt, in a dazed and foolish state.
  • 后读《孟蜀世家》 - Later, I read the *Biography of the Shu Kingdom of Meng*.
  • 宋太宗遣王全斌等伐蜀 - Emperor Taizong of Song sent Wang Quanbin and others to attack Shu.
  • 孟昶遣王昭远御之 - Meng Chang sent Wang Zhao Yuan to resist them.
  • 昭远好读兵书，以方略自许 - Wang Zhao Yuan liked to read military treatises and considered himself a master of strategy.
  • 兵始发成都，昶遣李昊等饯之 - When the troops first set out from Chengdu, Meng Chang sent Li Hao and others to see them off.
  • 昭远手执铁如意，自比诸葛亮 - Wang Zhao Yuan held an iron如意 (a decorative staff used by scholars and officials) and compared himself to Zhuge Liang.
  • 既而与全斌一战于三泉而败，再战于剑门而被擒 - Later, he fought a battle with Wang Quanbin at Sanquan and was defeated, and in another battle at Jianmen, he was captured.
  • 真是写成一笑！ - This is truly something to laugh at!

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 《异物志》 - A classical text documenting unusual or exotic things, particularly from southern China. This text is similar in function to Herodotus’ *Histories* or Pliny the Elder’s *Natural History* in the West.
  • 蚺蛇 - A large python, often associated with medicinal or magical properties in classical Chinese texts.
  • 土人 - Refers to the indigenous or local people of the southern regions. In classical Chinese, this term often carries a connotation of being outside the central cultural sphere.
  • 龙眼 - The lychee fruit, known for its sweet, juicy flesh and is a well-known fruit in southern China. It was highly valued in ancient China.
  • 《王莽传》 - A biography of Wang Mang, a usurper who briefly ruled the Western Han dynasty and established the Xin dynasty.
  • 剽摹古籍 - To copy or imitate ancient texts. This was a common literary practice in the Han dynasty.
  • 《大诰》 - A text from the Zhou dynasty, known for its severe and didactic tone, used by King Wu of Zhou to warn his subjects about the consequences of rebellion.
  • 《孟蜀世家》 - A section of the *Old Book of Tang* that records the history of the Later Shu kingdom, a short-lived kingdom in Sichuan during the Five Dynasties period.
  • 宋太宗 - Emperor Taizong of Song (939–997), the second emperor of the Song dynasty, known for his military campaigns to unify China.
  • 王全斌 - A general under Emperor Taizong who led the campaign against the Later Shu kingdom.
  • 孟昶 - The last ruler of the Later Shu kingdom, known for his literary and artistic interests but also for his military weakness.
  • 王昭远 - A general of the Later Shu kingdom, known for his overconfidence and eventual defeat in the campaign against the Song dynasty.
  • 诸葛亮 - Zhuge Liang, a famous strategist and statesman of the Three Kingdoms period, often considered the ideal general in Chinese history.
  • 雕面恶少年 - Literally 'fierce-faced evil youths,' a term used to describe warriors or soldiers who were feared for their ferocity.
  • 中原 - The Central Plains, the heartland of Chinese civilization and often the target of conquest in Chinese history.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • 南裔《异物志》：蚺蛇牙长六七寸，土人尤重之，云辟不祥，利远行。卖一枚，值牛数头。 This is a single, loosely connected sentence that describes the python's fang and its value. The clauses are not connected with conjunctions, and the meaning must be inferred through context.
  • 采龙眼 – The heading is brief and lacks a verb, which is typical in classical Chinese for section headings.
  • 龙眼枝甚柔脆，熟时赁惯手登采。恐其恣啖 – The sentence is incomplete and continues in the next section.

[411]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 然自古此等妄人，却又不少 - Concessive phrase meaning 'Indeed, from ancient times, there have been not a few such deluded individuals.'
  • 以妖术惑人 - 'Used witchcraft to deceive people.' 妖术 is a classical term for 'supernatural or magical arts' often associated with heresy or false religion.
  • 署置百官 - 'Appointed a hundred officials.' 署置 is a classical term for appointing or setting up positions.
  • 号其父曰太上皇 - 'Named his father as the 'Supreme Emperor.' 号 here is a classical term for 'to name or title someone.'
  • 斩于都市 - 'Was executed in the capital city.' 都市 can mean 'capital city' or 'public square.'
  • 临刑，人皆骂其自取族灭 - 'At the time of execution, people all cursed him for bringing about his family's destruction.' 族灭 is a classical term meaning 'extinction of the family' or 'total destruction of the clan.'
  • 太上皇蒙尘于外 - 'The Supreme Emperor is in exile.' 蒙尘 is a classical euphemism for being in exile or suffering misfortune, often used for emperors.
  • 征东、征西乱兵所害 - 'Killed by the rebellious troops of the East and West Generals.' 乱兵 refers to 'rebellious or disorderly soldiers.'
  • 朕躬虽存，复何聊赖 - 'Though my body still lives, what is the use?' 朕躬 is the emperor’s self-reference, 聊赖 means 'to have any purpose or meaning.'
  • 皇后何不达天命 - 'Why doesn’t the empress understand the will of Heaven?' 达天命 is a classical expression meaning 'to understand the Mandate of Heaven.'
  • 自古岂有不亡之国，不破之家耶 - 'Since ancient times, has there ever been a country that did not fall, or a family that did not collapse?' A rhetorical question expressing fatalism.
  • 行刑者以刀环筑其口 - 'The executioner struck his mouth with the blade's ring.' 刀环 is the circular part of a sword or dagger.
  • 朕即崩矣，终不改帝号也 - 'I am about to die, but I will never change my imperial title.' 崩 is a classical term for the death of an emperor.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 南燕 - A short-lived northern Chinese kingdom established by the Xianbei people in the late 4th to early 5th centuries. It is one of the 'Sixteen Kingdoms' of the Sixteen Kingdoms period.
  • 慕容德 - The founder and first emperor of the Southern Yan kingdom. He was a Xianbei leader who ruled during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
  • 建平四年 - The fourth year of the Jianping era of Emperor Murong De. This is the reign title of the Southern Yan dynasty.
  • 王始 - A historical figure who led a rebellion in the Southern Yan period. He claimed to be emperor and was executed for his rebellion.
  • 王莽 - A usurper of the Western Han dynasty who founded the Xin dynasty and is known for his failed attempt to restore the Zhou dynasty’s system of governance.
  • 天生德于予，汉兵其如予何 - A direct quote from Wang Mang, who claimed divine right to rule and dismissed the Han military as powerless against him.

Proper Nouns

  • 南燕 - Southern Yan (a kingdom in the Sixteen Kingdoms period)
  • 慕容德 - Murong De (founder of the Southern Yan)
  • 建平四年 - The fourth year of the Jianping era of Murong De
  • 王始 - Wang Shi (a rebel leader in Southern Yan)
  • 莱芜 - Laiwu (a place in present-day Shandong Province)
  • 太山莱芜谷 - The Laiwu Valley of Mount Tai (a mountain in Shandong)
  • 车骑将军 - General-in-Chief (a military title)
  • 桂阳王 - Prince Guiyang (a prince of the Southern Yan)
  • 太平皇帝 - Emperor of Peace (Wang Shi’s self-proclaimed title)
  • 太上皇 - Supreme Emperor (Wang Shi’s title for his father)
  • 征东将军 - General of the East (a military title)
  • 征西将军 - General of the West (a military title)
  • 赵氏 - Zhao (Wang Shi’s wife)
  • 王莽 - Wang Mang (a usurper of the Western Han dynasty)

[412]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 自谓卧龙复生 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'he called himself the reincarnation of Zhuge Liang (卧龙).'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 嘉泰开禧 - The years 1201-1207 during the reign of Emperor Ningzong of Song.
  • 郭倪 - A high-ranking official during the Song Dynasty.
  • 卧龙 - A nickname for Zhuge Liang, a famous strategist and chancellor during the Three Kingdoms period.
  • 三顾频烦，两朝开济 - Lines from Du Fu's poem 'Chancellor of Shu,' praising Zhuge Liang's loyalty and service.
  • 陈景俊 - A military official during the Song Dynasty.
  • 军漕 - A military official responsible for logistics.
  • 木牛流马 - A metaphor for complex machinery or strategies, often associated with Zhuge Liang.
  • 富平之败 - The Battle of Fuping, a significant military defeat during the Song Dynasty.
  • 彭法传 - Another official present at the banquet.

Proper Nouns

  • 嘉泰开禧 - The years 1201-1207.
  • 郭倪 - A high-ranking official.
  • 陈景俊 - A military official.
  • 彭法传 - Another official.
  • 富平之败 - The Battle of Fuping.

[413]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 值黄霉 - Refers to a period of dampness and mold, often associated with a time when things can go wrong or become unclean.
  • 毅然难犯 - Resolute and difficult to offend, a term often used to describe a person who is strong-willed and principled.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 鄞县 - A county in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 诸生 - A term used for scholars or students in the imperial examination system.
  • 主妾 - The mistress of the household.
  • 斋中 - The study or living room.
  • 操刀 - Holding a knife, often used metaphorically to indicate a threat.

Proper Nouns

  • 鄞县 - A county in Zhejiang Province, China.
  • 陆世科 - The main character, a scholar or student.
  • 主妾 - The mistress of the household.
  • 斋中 - The study or living room.

[414]

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 后登万历己丑进士 - A formal statement indicating that the person passed the imperial examination in the year 1589 (Wanli Jichou) during the Ming dynasty.
  • 仕至大理卿 - Indicates the highest official position achieved, which is the Minister of Justice (大理卿).

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 外史氏曰 - A conventional phrase used by commentators or historians to offer their own commentary or interpretation on a historical or literary matter.
  • 亦尝载此事 - Also recorded this matter or also recounted this event. The term '尝' (cháng) implies that the source has previously mentioned the event.
  • 并 不详其生平为明季遗老也 - Nor did it detail that his life was that of a remnant of the late Ming dynasty. The term '遗老' (yí lǎo) refers to a scholar or official who remained loyal to the fallen dynasty and refused to serve the new one.
  • 事由前定 - The matter was predestined. A phrase that reflects a belief in fate or divine will.
  • 不肯屈节于新朝 - Would not bend his knee to the new dynasty. A metaphor for refusing to serve the new ruling power.
  • 龚诩之不仕成祖 - Gong Xu's refusal to serve the Yongle Emperor. This refers to a historical figure, Gong Xu, who refused to serve the Yongle Emperor of the Ming dynasty.
  • 谓恐负金川门一恸耳 - He said he feared betraying the sorrow at the Golden Gate. '金川门' (Jīnchuān Mén) is a historical reference, possibly to the fall of the Ming dynasty, and '一恸' (yī tòng) means a single cry or lament.
  • 古来忠臣孝子，岂肯以时命之故移其志哉 - Since ancient times, loyal ministers and filial sons would not change their resolve because of the times or fate. This is a rhetorical question emphasizing steadfastness of character.
  • 腹语 - A term meaning 'speaking from the abdomen,' referring to the phenomenon of someone speaking without a head, as in the case of decapitation, where the voice is thought to emerge from the body despite the head being severed.
  • 先生临刑不屈，首已坠，而声从项出 - The scholar did not yield to execution, his head had already fallen, but his voice came from his neck. This is a literal description of a phenomenon that may be interpreted as supernatural.
  • 《聊斋志异》言 - The *Liaozhai Zhiyi* (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio) says... This is a reference to a famous collection of supernatural and folk tales by Pu Songling.
  • 贾雍为豫章太守 - Jia Yong was the governor of Yuzhang. '豫章' (Yuzhang) is an ancient name for modern-day Nanchang in Jiangxi province.
  • 丧元 - Lost his head or lost his head (in battle). A euphemistic way of saying 'was decapitated.'
  • 挟马归营 - Rode back to camp with the horse. This suggests that the decapitated person was still able to ride, implying a supernatural or miraculous event.
  • 有头佳乎？无头佳乎？ - Is it better to have a head or not to have a head? A rhetorical question posed by Jia Yong.
  • 无头亦佳 - Even without a head, it is still good. This is Jia Yong's reply, indicating his acceptance of his fate.
  • 神全 - Spirit intact or spirit whole. This suggests that the person's spirit or life force remained undiminished despite physical death.
  • 唐花卿为剑南节度使 - Tang Huaqing was the military governor of Jian'nan. '剑南' (Jian'nan) refers to a region in Sichuan, and '节度使' (jié dù shǐ) is a military governor during the Tang dynasty.
  • 段子璋 - Duan Zizhang, a historical figure mentioned in the text.
  • 临溪沃盥 - Washed his hands by the stream. This is a ritual act, often associated with purification.
  • 浣女 - Washer woman or washer girl, someone who washes clothes or herself by the river.
  • 无头何以洗盥？ - How can you wash your hands without a head? A rhetorical question indicating disbelief or confusion.
  • 闻言遂仆 - Upon hearing this, she collapsed. This describes the washer woman's reaction to the miraculous sight.
  • 神散之验也 - This is the evidence of a dispersed spirit. This suggests that the loss of the head corresponds to the loss of the spirit or soul.
